nativeRange.habitat: The Starry Blenny inhabits shallow, protected inshore reefs, estuaries, and weedy sand areas around rocky outcrops, typically at depths of 1 to 15 metres. In the wild it is most often found perched on rubble margins and coral formations, where it grazes on algae film.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock for perching and grazing
open sand bed with rubble margins

diet.notes: Has been documented as a near-constant algae grazer; does best in mature aquariums with abundant film/turf algae. Generally advised to supplement with dried seaweed (nori), Spirulina, and herbivore preparations to prevent it from nipping at corals when underfed.

avoidWith: lawnmower blenny and other algae-grazing blennies
conspecifics in modest tanks (unless a mated pair)

summary: The Starry Blenny (Salarias ramosus) is a mottled brown fish dusted with white star-like spots, valued in reef aquariums for its near-constant algae grazing. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe with caution, it does best in mature tanks with ample film algae and a secured lid.
careNotes: The Starry Blenny is generally considered a hardy, peaceful fish that spends most of its time perched on rockwork and hopping between grazing spots. Although broadly accepted as reef-safe, it has been known to nip at stony coral polyps and clam mantles when film and turf algae run short, so maintaining consistent algae growth or supplementing daily with dried seaweed is generally advised. The species tends to do best when kept singly; it may harass other similarly-shaped blennies such as the Lawnmower Blenny, Sailfin Blenny, and Bicolor Blenny, and two specimens in a modest tank are likely to conflict unless they form a confirmed mated pair. Like most blennies in the Salarias genus, it is a strong jumper, making a tightly-sealed lid or mesh cover essential. A tank of at least 200 litres with ample algae-covered rockwork gives the fish enough territory to graze and reduces the likelihood of coral-nipping.
breedingNotes: The Starry Blenny has been reported to spawn in captivity on occasion. It is oviparous, attaching demersal adhesive eggs to surfaces via filamentous pedestals. The resulting planktonic larvae are difficult to raise outside of dedicated larviculture setups, and the species is not commonly captive-bred.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source — care level, water parameters, temperament, diet, reef-safe status. Note: 30 US gal minimum quoted by retailer was treated as low-end; larger value chosen per playbook tank-size rule.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (14 cm TL), depth range (1–15 m), taxonomy (Blenniidae, Blenniiformes), native range (Western Central Pacific), and wild temperature range (25.3–29.2 °C).

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Tertiary hobbyist source — confirmed jumping/escape risk (high, lid required), peaceful temperament, herbivore diet, lifespan up to ~6 years in captivity, and a note that grazing behavior may occasionally disturb corals.

fish.breedingNotes: The planktonic larvae are difficult to raise and the species is not commonly bred in home aquaria.
